query:
UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
‚Ä¢ Inspect all power tools before use: check cords for damage, guards for proper function, blades/bits for wear, and switches for proper operation. Damaged tools must be tagged out and removed from service.
‚Ä¢ Guards are there for a reason ‚Äî never remove, bypass, or tie back a guard on any power tool. Circular saws, table saws, grinders, and routers all require functioning guards.
‚Ä¢ Use the right tool for the job. Don''t use a circular saw when a reciprocating saw is needed. Don''t force a tool to do something it wasn''t designed for ‚Äî that''s when injuries happen.
‚Ä¢ Secure your workpiece. Use clamps, vises, or a stable work surface. Never hold a small piece with one hand while cutting with the other. Never cut toward your body.
‚Ä¢ Disconnect power before changing blades, bits, or making adjustments. For cordless tools, remove the battery. For pneumatic tools, disconnect the air line.
‚Ä¢ Wear appropriate PPE for each tool: safety glasses (always), hearing protection (for loud tools), dust mask (for cutting/grinding), and cut-resistant gloves (when appropriate ‚Äî but never with rotating tools where gloves can catch).
‚Ä¢ Real-world example: A framer was using a circular saw with a cracked guard that didn''t retract properly. He was making a plunge cut when the blade kicked back and cut through his thigh, severing an artery. He nearly bled out before reaching the hospital. A functioning guard would have prevented the kickback.

Discussion Questions:
1. Have all power tools been inspected today?
2. Are all guards in place and functioning on our saws and grinders?
3. Does everyone have the right PPE for the tools they''re using?

Sign-off: Respect the tool. Check the guard. Secure the work. Every time.' WHERE `id` = 19;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
‚Ä¢ Poor housekeeping is the root cause of most slips, trips, and falls on construction sites. A clean job site is a safe job site ‚Äî and it shows professionalism to clients and inspectors.
‚Ä¢ Keep walkways, stairs, and access routes clear of debris, tools, cords, and materials at all times. If you can''t walk through an area safely, clean it before continuing work.
‚Ä¢ Secure all cords and hoses. Run them overhead when possible, or use cord covers/ramps on the ground. Loose cords across walkways are one of the most common trip hazards.
‚Ä¢ Clean up spills immediately ‚Äî water, oil, hydraulic fluid, and concrete slurry all create slip hazards. Use absorbent materials for oil spills and mark the area until it''s fully cleaned.
‚Ä¢ Stack materials properly: lumber on flat, stable surfaces with stickers between layers. Don''t stack materials higher than you can safely reach. Secure stacks against tipping with straps or braces.
‚Ä¢ Nails in scrap lumber are a serious puncture hazard. Bend nails over or remove them immediately. Designate a scrap pile and clean it regularly. Step-on nail injuries can cause infections and tetanus.
‚Ä¢ Real-world example: A laborer tripped over a scrap 2x4 that was left in a walkway and fell onto a pile of lumber with exposed nails. He suffered a puncture wound in his hand and a sprained ankle. The scrap had been there for two days ‚Äî no one had cleaned it up. A 30-second cleanup would have prevented the injury.

Discussion Questions:
1. What areas of our job site need cleanup right now?
2. Are all walkways and access routes clear?
3. Who is responsible for end-of-day cleanup, and is it happening?

Sign-off: Clean as you go. A messy site is a dangerous site.' WHERE `id` = 20;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
‚Ä¢ Struck-by hazards are the second leading cause of death in construction. Workers can be struck by falling objects, flying objects, swinging objects, or rolling objects.
‚Ä¢ Falling objects: Secure all tools and materials at height. Use tool lanyards, toe boards on scaffolds, and debris nets. Never throw materials from elevation ‚Äî use a controlled lowering method.
‚Ä¢ Flying objects: Wear safety glasses at all times. When using powder-actuated tools, grinders, or saws, ensure bystanders are at a safe distance or behind barriers.
‚Ä¢ Swinging objects: Stay clear of crane loads, excavator swing radius, and any suspended materials. Use tag lines to control loads ‚Äî never guide them with your hands.
‚Ä¢ Rolling objects: Chock wheels on vehicles and equipment when parked on grades. Secure round materials (pipe, conduit, rebar bundles) against rolling with wedges or racks.
‚Ä¢ Establish and enforce exclusion zones around overhead work, crane operations, and material handling areas. Use barricades, caution tape, and signage to keep unauthorized workers out.
‚Ä¢ Real-world example: A worker was walking below a scaffold when a hammer fell from the platform above. It struck him on the shoulder, fracturing his collarbone. The hammer had no lanyard and there was no toe board on the scaffold. Both would have prevented the incident.

Discussion Questions:
1. Where are the struck-by hazards on our job site today?
2. Are all tools at height secured with lanyards?
3. Are exclusion zones properly marked around overhead work?

Sign-off: Look up, look around, and secure everything that could fall or fly.' WHERE `id` = 21;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
‚Ä¢ Caught-in/between hazards occur when a worker is caught, crushed, or compressed between two or more objects. This includes trench cave-ins, unguarded machinery, and being caught between equipment and structures.
‚Ä¢ Never place any body part between moving and stationary objects. This includes between a forklift and a wall, between a crane load and a structure, or between closing doors/gates.
‚Ä¢ Loose clothing, long hair, jewelry, and drawstrings can be caught in rotating machinery. Tuck in shirts, tie back hair, remove jewelry, and cut drawstrings before working near any rotating equipment.
‚Ä¢ Trench cave-ins are a caught-in hazard ‚Äî always use proper shoring, sloping, or trench boxes as discussed in the Excavation safety topic.
‚Ä¢ When working between vehicles or equipment and fixed objects, ensure the operator knows you''re there. Make eye contact. Use a spotter. Never assume the operator can see you.
‚Ä¢ Lockout/Tagout is critical for preventing caught-in injuries during maintenance. Never reach into machinery without proper LOTO procedures.
‚Ä¢ Real-world example: A worker was guiding a concrete wall panel into place when it shifted. His hand was caught between the panel and the steel column. He lost two fingers. A guide rope instead of hands-on guidance would have kept him at a safe distance.

Discussion Questions:
1. What caught-in/between hazards exist on our job site today?
2. Is anyone working near rotating machinery or between heavy objects?
3. Are all workers wearing appropriate clothing (no loose items)?

Sign-off: Keep your hands, body, and clothing clear of pinch points. Use tools, not fingers.' WHERE `id` = 22;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
‚Ä¢ A confined space has limited entry/exit, is not designed for continuous occupancy, and is large enough for a worker to enter. Examples: tanks, vaults, manholes, crawl spaces, and attics with limited access.
‚Ä¢ A permit-required confined space also has one or more hazards: atmospheric (low oxygen, toxic gas, explosive atmosphere), engulfment (grain, sand, water), entrapment, or other serious hazards.
‚Ä¢ Before entry: test the atmosphere with a calibrated 4-gas monitor (O2, LEL, CO, H2S). Oxygen must be between 19.5% and 23.5%. Any toxic gas reading above the PEL requires ventilation or respiratory protection.
‚Ä¢ The entry permit must list: the space, hazards, authorized entrants, attendant, entry supervisor, atmospheric readings, rescue procedures, and communication methods.
‚Ä¢ An attendant must be stationed outside the confined space at all times. Their only job is to monitor entrants and summon rescue if needed. They must NEVER enter the space to attempt rescue.
‚Ä¢ Rescue plans must be in place before entry. This can be self-rescue, non-entry rescue (retrieval system with tripod and winch), or entry rescue by a trained rescue team. Most confined space fatalities involve would-be rescuers.
‚Ä¢ Real-world example: A plumber entered a manhole to repair a pipe. He was overcome by hydrogen sulfide gas and collapsed. His coworker entered to help and also collapsed. Both died. A 4-gas monitor would have detected the H2S before entry. The attendant should never have entered.

Discussion Questions:
1. Are there any confined spaces on this job site?
2. Do we have a 4-gas monitor and is it calibrated?
3. What is our rescue plan for confined space emergencies?

Sign-off: Test the air. Post an attendant. Have a rescue plan. No shortcuts in confined spaces.' WHERE `id` = 23;

UPDATE `safetyTopics` SET `content` = 'Key Points:
‚Ä¢ Back injuries are the most common injury in construction and the leading cause of lost work days. Most are preventable with proper technique and planning.
‚Ä¢ Proper lifting technique: Stand close to the load, feet shoulder-width apart. Bend at the knees, not the waist. Keep your back straight and core tight. Grip the load firmly. Lift with your legs in a smooth motion. Keep the load close to your body. Never twist while lifting ‚Äî move your feet to turn.
‚Ä¢ Know your limits. NIOSH recommends a maximum of 51 pounds for ideal lifting conditions. For awkward positions, overhead work, or repetitive lifting, the safe limit is much lower. If it''s too heavy, get help or use equipment.
‚Ä¢ Use mechanical aids whenever possible: dollies, hand trucks, forklifts, hoists, and team lifts. The goal is to eliminate manual lifting, not just do it with better form.
‚Ä¢ Repetitive lifting causes cumulative damage. Rotate tasks throughout the day to give your back a break. Stretch before starting physical work ‚Äî focus on hamstrings, hip flexors, and lower back.
‚Ä¢ Awkward positions are as dangerous as heavy loads. Avoid reaching overhead, bending sideways, or lifting from the ground repeatedly. Reposition materials to waist height when possible.
‚Ä¢ Real-world example: A framer was carrying sheets of plywood overhead by himself ‚Äî each sheet weighs about 70 lbs. After the third trip, he felt a pop in his lower back. He herniated a disc and was out for 3 months, requiring physical therapy. Using a dolly or two-person carry would have prevented the injury.

Discussion Questions:
1. What heavy lifting tasks are planned for today?
2. Do we have the right equipment (dollies, hoists) available?
3. Has everyone stretched and warmed up before starting physical work?

Sign-off: Your back has to last your whole career. Lift smart, use equipment, and ask for help.' WHERE `id` = 24;
